justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Latency histogram gap explicitly identified as blocking SLA monitoring — called CRITICAL and linked to inability to measure/alert on 99% < 10s SLA. (2) Undifferentiated failure counter identified — S3 failures lumped into requests_failed_total, cannot distinguish S3 outage from validation failures. (3) Specific recommendations: latency histogram for p99 alerting, failure_reason label equivalent (S3 errors broken out). (4) File size tracking noted as CRITICAL rather than nice-to-have, which is a minor overcall but does not trigger a failure mode since it wasn't recommended as a must-fix blocker for the verdict. (5) Verdict is NOT READY with clear must-fix items.

observations.text: Latency histogram gap clearly identified as CRITICAL and directly connected to SLA enforceability — 'SLA is effectively unenforceable without instrumentation'
Failure counter conflation explicitly called out — 'S3 errors lumped into requests_failed_total — cannot distinguish S3 outage from validation failures'
Specific metric recommendations provided: latency histogram for p99, breaking out S3 errors from the failed counter
Verdict is NOT READY with deployment_ready: false, correctly blocking on observability gaps
Excellent resilience analysis goes beyond the rubric — identifies circuit breaker gap, timeout × retry worst-case math (90s), slow client DoS, and memory buffering concerns
Operational readiness section is thorough — identifies missing runbook, on-call documentation gaps, and monitoring blind spots
File size distribution metric labeled CRITICAL rather than nice-to-have — rubric expects this as a lower-priority recommendation, not a critical gap
Failure reason label recommendation is implicit (break out S3 errors) rather than explicitly naming a failure_reason label with specific values (validation|s3_error|timeout)
